questions.question: Why cut raw stock before CNC?
questions.answer: The saw gives the CNC a controlled blank. Leave finish allowance so the mill can face clean material, but not so much that you waste cycle time and stock.

questions.question: Why does tool stickout matter?
questions.answer: Every extra bit of stickout acts like a longer lever. Keep enough reach for the feature and holder clearance, then stop. Short and rigid beats long and hopeful.

questions.question: What should I check before cycle start?
questions.answer: Trace the program block by block: work offset, tool and length offset, spindle direction, coolant, safe approach, cutting path, and retract. Know where the tool will go before you press start.

questions.question: Why inspect the first article?
questions.answer: The first part proves the setup. Check it before the remaining material becomes inventory—or repeated scrap. Record the result and the correction.

questions.question: Why keep material certifications?
questions.answer: A cert connects the part to alloy, heat lot, chemistry, and properties. Keep that chain intact so you can prove what the customer received.

questions.question: What should I do after scrap?
questions.answer: Contain the problem, identify the cause, change the process, and verify the correction. Scrap is tuition only when the lesson changes the next part.

questions.question: When should I hire?
questions.answer: Hire when recurring work exceeds your reliable capacity and cash can carry the wage through a slow stretch. Define the work and the training plan before filling the chair.

questions.question: What makes a good handoff?
questions.answer: Write what ran, what comes next, and what looks unusual. Include counts, offsets, tool life, inspection status, and the exact point where the next person starts.

questions.question: How do I grow an operator?
questions.answer: Explain the standard, demonstrate it, let them perform it, then verify understanding. Give responsibility in steps and teach the reason behind every control point.

questions.question: Should I buy another machine?
questions.answer: Only when demand, staffing, floor space, tooling, and cash support it. Utilization without profitable work is an expensive way to make noise.

questions.question: How should I price a job?
questions.answer: Price material, setup, cycle time, inspection, outside services, risk, overhead, and margin. A busy shop can still go broke when quotes ignore the whole route.

questions.question: When have I outgrown this facility?
questions.answer: When safe flow, reliable delivery, people development, and profitable demand are constrained by the building—not by unfinished process discipline.
